questions.question_text: The Connecticut Common Interest Ownership Act (CIOA) governs condominiums, planned communities, and cooperatives created on or after:
questions.explanation: CGS § 47-214: CIOA applies to common interest communities created on or after January 1, 1984. Older condominiums remain under the 1976 Condominium Act (chapter 825), though certain CIOA provisions also apply to pre-1984 communities.

questions.question_text: When selling a unit in a CIOA community, the seller must provide the buyer with a:
questions.explanation: The seller must provide a resale certificate containing association financial information, governing documents, and special assessments. This gives the buyer essential information about the community's financial health.

questions.question_text: After receiving a CIOA resale certificate, the buyer has how many days to review and cancel?
questions.explanation: Under CGS § 47-270(c), the purchase contract is voidable until five business days after the resale certificate is hand-delivered (seven business days if mailed), or until conveyance, whichever occurs first. The 15-day cancellation right applies to public offering statements for new units (§ 47-269).

questions.question_text: A unit owner selling in a CIOA community requests a resale certificate from the association. It must be furnished within:
questions.explanation: CGS § 47-270(b): after the unit owner's request and payment of the statutory fee, the association must furnish the certificate within ten business days (three business days where an expedited fee applies). The buyer may then void the contract until five business days after delivery.

questions.question_text: A CIOA association forecloses its lien for a unit owner's unpaid common expenses. As against the unit's first mortgage, the association's lien:
questions.correct_answer: Has priority for up to 9 months of assessments
distractors: Is always junior to the first mortgage regardless of amount
Has priority for the entire delinquency no matter how large
Shares equal pro rata priority with the first mortgage lien
questions.explanation: CGS § 47-258(b): the lien is prior to all liens except pre-declaration liens, first/second mortgages recorded before delinquency, and tax liens — but it primes even first and second mortgages for nine months of common-expense assessments plus the association's costs and attorney's fees.

questions.question_text: Connecticut's fair housing law is enforced by the:
questions.correct_answer: Commission on Human Rights and Opportunities
distractors: Office of the Connecticut Attorney General
questions.explanation: The CHRO enforces Connecticut's fair housing law (Connecticut Human Rights and Opportunities Act). CHRO investigates complaints, mediates, holds hearings, and can award damages.

questions.question_text: A landlord refuses to rent to a tenant who pays with a Section 8 housing voucher. In Connecticut, this is:
questions.correct_answer: Illegal discrimination based on lawful source of income
distractors: Legal because landlords can choose payment methods
Legal unless the unit has more than 4 units
Illegal only in municipalities with fair rent commissions
questions.explanation: CGS § 46a-64c protects lawful source of income, including Section 8 vouchers and public assistance. Rejection is lawful only for legitimate reasons such as insufficient income (§ 46a-64c(b)(5)); narrow owner-occupied exemptions aside, the protection applies statewide.

questions.question_text: Connecticut's fair housing law differs from federal law in that Connecticut:
questions.correct_answer: Has a narrower owner-occupied rental exemption
distractors: Protects fewer classes than federal law does
Applies its protections to rental housing only
Exempts all single-family home sales from coverage
questions.explanation: CGS § 46a-64c(b) exempts only room rentals in an owner-occupied home and owner-occupied two-family houses (up to four units for familial status only) — narrower than the federal Mrs. Murphy exemption for owner-occupied buildings of up to four units. Connecticut also adds protected classes.

questions.question_text: A fair housing complaint must be filed with CHRO within how many days of the discriminatory act?
questions.explanation: CGS § 46a-82(f)(2): for acts occurring on or after October 1, 2021, a CHRO complaint must be filed within 300 days of the alleged discriminatory act (extended from 180 days). A federal complaint to HUD may be filed within one year.

questions.question_text: Which of the following is a Connecticut-specific protected class not covered by federal fair housing law?
questions.explanation: Connecticut protects additional classes beyond federal law including veteran status, lawful source of income, gender identity/expression, sexual orientation, age, marital status, and domestic violence victim status.

questions.question_text: Under Connecticut's adoption of UETA, electronic signatures on real estate contracts are:
distractors: Not yet recognized for real estate transactions
questions.explanation: Connecticut adopted UETA, giving electronic signatures the same legal effect as handwritten signatures for real estate contracts, disclosures, and agency agreements.

questions.question_text: For an electronic signature to be valid on a real estate contract in Connecticut, both parties must:
questions.correct_answer: Consent to conducting the transaction electronically
distractors: File a copy with the town clerk electronically
questions.explanation: UETA requires mutual consent to electronic transactions. Both parties must agree to conduct the transaction electronically — one party cannot force electronic execution on an unwilling party.

questions.question_text: Connecticut's UETA does NOT apply to which of the following?
questions.explanation: UETA does not apply to wills, testamentary trusts, or certain UCC transactions. Real estate contracts, disclosures, and agency agreements can all be executed electronically.

questions.question_text: A buyer wants to sign a purchase contract electronically but the seller insists on paper. Under UETA:
questions.correct_answer: The transaction must proceed on paper
distractors: The buyer may compel electronic signatures anyway
A court must resolve the disagreement over format
The listing agent selects the signing method used
questions.explanation: CGS § 1-270(b): Connecticut UETA applies only between parties that have each agreed to conduct the transaction electronically, and consent cannot be forced. Because the seller declines, the contract must be executed on paper.

questions.question_text: Which of the following Connecticut real estate documents can be executed with an electronic signature under UETA?
distractors: A testamentary trust transferring real property
A last will devising real property
questions.explanation: Agency disclosures, purchase contracts, listing agreements, and most real estate documents can be signed electronically under UETA. Wills, testamentary trusts, and certain UCC transactions are excluded.

questions.question_text: Under PA 25-168 (effective July 2026), real estate wholesalers in Connecticut must:
questions.correct_answer: Register with the Department of Consumer Protection
distractors: Obtain a full real estate broker's license from the state
Complete 60 hours of state-approved pre-license education
Pass the Connecticut real estate salesperson licensing exam
questions.explanation: PA 25-168 requires wholesalers to register with DCP (not obtain a full license). The registration fee is $285 biennial. This is a lighter requirement than full licensure.

questions.question_text: A wholesale contract in Connecticut must give the seller a cancellation window of:
questions.explanation: PA 25-168 requires that sellers be given a 3-business-day cancellation window on wholesale contracts, providing time to review terms and consult advisors.

questions.question_text: The maximum time between contract signing and closing for a wholesale transaction in Connecticut is:
questions.explanation: PA 25-168 prohibits wholesalers from setting a closing date more than 90 days after the contract is signed. This prevents deals from dragging on indefinitely.

questions.question_text: The biennial registration fee for a Connecticut real estate wholesaler is:
questions.explanation: The wholesaler registration fee is $285, paid biennially (every 2 years). This is separate from the $80 salesperson application fee or $120 broker application fee.

questions.question_text: A wholesaler assigns a contract to an investor 45 days after the seller signed. The seller wants to cancel. Under PA 25-168:
questions.correct_answer: The 3-business-day cancellation period has already passed
distractors: The seller can cancel at any time before closing
The seller has 10 days from the assignment date
The seller can cancel because 90 days haven't elapsed
questions.explanation: The 3-business-day cancellation window runs from the date the seller signs the contract. At 45 days, the cancellation period has long passed. The 90-day limit is for the closing deadline, not cancellation.
